title: GPs want to monitor kids’ first five years
full_text: Other Editions 05 May 2026 TheNation GPs want to monitor kids’ first five years The Australian Robyn Riley 5 May 2026 566 words English AUSTLN 7 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Australia’s 50,000 general practitioners want every child to have a free annual health check until the age of five. The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners, the peak body for general practice, says the hour-long, Medicare-funded visit is critical to children’s long-term health and social outcomes. The RACGP wants an ­inclusive program of longer consults for every child for the first 2000 days of their life. If funded in next Tuesday’s federal budget, the initiative would be an Australian first. The RACGP says the comprehensive health assessments could help identify developmental concerns. They would also help GPs deliver Thriving Kids, a new national program to support children aged eight and under with developmental delay and autism outside the National Disability Insurance Scheme, but also be a general health check for every child. The call comes as experts warn the health of children is at a crossroads – the worst in a generation – but that early intervention can move the dial. On Monday, the Murdoch Children’s Research Institute revealed a bold four-point plan to reverse the trend. Experts say one in four Australian children are obese or overweight, they are sleeping one hour less a night, and they are not moving. MCRI director Kathryn North says for the first time in a generation children’s health is worse than that of their parents. She says MCRI wants to reverse that trend, “at scale, speed and low cost”. The RACGP said the cost of its proposal to extend the Medicare Benefits Schedule to support the annual checks for the first five years of a child’s life would be $160m over four years. RACGP president Michael Wright said children who receive support early are more likely to engage positively in school, reducing the need for more costly extra educational or healthcare interventions. “The first 2000 days of a child’s life shape their future,” Dr Wright said. “GPs are uniquely placed to identify developmental concerns early, support families and co-ordinate care, but they need the right tools and funding. This investment would ­ensure children get the support they need at the right time, rather than waiting until challenges become harder and more expensive to address.” Dr Tim Jones chairs the RACGP’s Specific Interest Child and Young Person’s Health group. The Tasmanian GP said early intervention was crucial and that all families should feel the health system understood the needs their children had at different ages and stages. “This proposal is for every child and for every parent in Australia, because during even the most typical of developmental journeys there will be stressed parents at some ­stages, and there doesn’t have to be a difference in development for families to feel like they’re entitled to get some extra care and support,” he said. Dr Jones said the proposal was for the first five years of a child’s life because this was when development was most dynamic. “Small changes make big impacts after five if you have found families early and given them timely support,” he said. “Change is still possible (after the age of five), but it’s a lot harder because kids’ patterns of learning, doing things and understanding the world have started to become more formed.” The Australian-20260505-Australian-7-000500670577 Nationwide News Pty Ltd. summary: The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners is advocating for annual free health checks for children up to five years old, funded by Medicare. This initiative aims to improve long-term health outcomes and identify developmental concerns early. The proposal, costing $160 million over four years, seeks to address alarming trends in children's health, including rising obesity rates and inadequate sleep.
publication:
Seniors to cop a $1600 health cover hike
Daily Telegraph05 May 2026279 wordsJulie Cross

Older Australians are facing private health insurance price hikes of more than $1600 from April next year, under the Albanese government’s plan to scrap higher rebates for over-65s.
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: RACGP calls for annual Medicare-funded health checks for children until age five.
Proposal aims to identify developmental issues early and support overall child health.
Cost of the initiative is estimated at $160 million over four years.
relevance: This initiative highlights the financial implications of health policies that may affect future healthcare costs and insurance models in Australia.
